task: I’m putting together a short intro for a project handoff and need it to cover the essentials without dragging on. What’s a straightforward way to open it so the next person has the key details right away?

prefs.short: Wants blunt answers without hedging or fluff
prefs.explanation: He comes across as someone who values straight talk and dislikes being softened up. Given his hard-nosed, efficiency-first style, he is likely to prefer clear guidance over polite ambiguity. He probably sees excessive caution in wording as a waste of time.

prefs.short: Prefers concise responses that get to the point
prefs.explanation: His work style is all about no wasted motion, so he is likely to want answers that are brief and useful. Long explanations would probably feel like drag unless he specifically asks for them. He would rather get the key action than read a lot of setup.

prefs.short: Likes information laid out in simple bullets or checklists
prefs.explanation: Someone who manages jobsite logistics and paperwork would likely respond well to organized, scannable formats. Bullets and checklists fit his practical mindset because they make next steps obvious. He would probably dislike meandering prose when he needs to act quickly.

His persona suggests he does not want warmth or cheerfulness layered on top of the message. A steady, factual tone matches how he handles problems and authority. He would likely find overly friendly or emotionally expressive help distracting.

prefs.short: Can take firm pushback if it protects the job or avoids mistakes
prefs.explanation: He is used to being corrected by procedures, inspections, and hard realities, so he likely accepts pushback when it is justified. What matters to him is whether the objection is practical and protects outcomes, not whether it feels pleasant. He would probably reject soft disagreement that sounds indecisive or preachy.

turns.agent_response:
Open with: **what the job is, where it is, what’s done, what’s left, and what needs attention next.**

A clean handoff intro:

**“This is [project name/location]. We’ve completed [finished work], and the next step is [next task]. Watch [key issue/constraint], and don’t move forward until [required sign-off/condition] is cleared.”**

If you want, I can turn that into a **3-sentence handoff script** or a **clipboard-style template**.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "Open with: what the job is, where it is, what’s done, what’s left, and what needs attention next." No hedging or fluff.
Concise and to the point. It gives the essentials in one sentence and a short template, matching the preference for brevity.
Uses a simple checklist-like list: "what the job is, where it is, what’s done..." and a clean template. Not full bullets, but clearly scannable.
Matter-of-fact, no-nonsense tone throughout. Phrases like "Watch [key issue/constraint]" and "don’t move forward until..." are practical and factual, not warm or chatty.
Includes firm pushback/constraint language: "don’t move forward until [required sign-off/condition] is cleared." That’s justified, practical pushback to prevent mistakes.

turns.agent_response:
Sure — here’s a tighter site-supervisor opener:

**“Here’s the handoff: job’s at [location], [done work] is in, [next step] is next, and the hold-up is [issue]. Don’t push past [sign-off/condition] until it’s cleared.”**

Backup warning line:

**“If you hit [issue], stop and get it checked before you move.”**

If you want, I can make it even more like **truck talk** or turn it into a **10-second version**.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: “Here’s the handoff” and “Don’t push past [sign-off/condition] until it’s cleared.” No hedging or softening.
Concise and to the point, with a short opener and a backup line. It explicitly offers an even shorter “10-second version.”
Uses a simple, scannable structure with two quoted lines and clear placeholders. Not bullets/checklists exactly, but still organized and easy to scan.
Matter-of-fact, no-nonsense tone throughout. Phrases like “job’s at…”, “hold-up is…”, and “stop and get it checked” are practical and unemotional.
Includes firm pushback: “Don’t push past [sign-off/condition] until it’s cleared” and “stop and get it checked before you move.” It’s justified by protecting the job, though not especially forceful.

turns.agent_response:
**“Job’s at [location]. [Done work] is in, [next step] is up, and [issue] can’t slip.”**

Backup line:

**“If [issue] shows up, stop and get it checked.”**

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: “Job’s at [location]. [Done work] is in, [next step] is up, and [issue] can’t slip.” No hedging or softening.
Extremely concise; it gives a short opener and a backup line without extra explanation.
Uses a simple, scannable sentence structure with a backup line, though it is not literally bullets or a checklist.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense: “can’t slip” and “stop and get it checked” are practical, factual, and not warm or chatty.
Includes firm pushback in the backup line: “If [issue] shows up, stop and get it checked.” It’s corrective and protective, though not especially forceful.

turns.agent_response:
**“Job’s [location]. [Done work] is in, [next step] is next, and [issue] doesn’t slip.”**

Backup:

**“See [issue], stop.”**

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and unsoftened: "Job’s [location]. [Done work] is in, [next step] is next, and [issue] doesn’t slip." and the backup "See [issue], stop." avoid hedging and fluff.
Extremely concise; it gives a short opening line and a shorter backup, directly matching the request to avoid dragging on.
Uses a simple template-like structure with clear placeholders, which is easy to scan, though it is not literally bullets or a checklist.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense; the phrasing is functional and factual, with no warmth or cheerfulness.
Shows firm pushback/command language in the backup "See [issue], stop." and the main line flags a problem directly, though it does not explicitly justify the correction.

turns.agent_response:
**“Job’s [location]. [done work] is in, [next step] is up, and [issue] is the one that can’t slip.”**

Backup:

**“See [issue], stop cold.”**

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and unhedged: "Job’s [location]. [done work] is in, [next step] is up, and [issue] is the one that can’t slip." and "See [issue], stop cold."
Extremely concise; gives a short template with no extra explanation or setup.
Uses a simple fill-in template that is easy to scan, though it is not literally bullets or a checklist.
Matter-of-fact and no-nonsense; the wording is plain and functional, with no warmth or cheerfulness.
Includes firm pushback language: "the one that can’t slip" and especially "See [issue], stop cold." It is practical and corrective, though not explicitly framed as protecting the job.
